Criteria Description: No distinct architecture identified.
Architecture chosen contradicts the data type (e.g., using a basic MLP for complex image data, or RNNs for non-sequential data).
Uses deprecated architectures (e.g., AlexNet or vanilla RNNs) without a specific justification for why they are preferred over modern baselines.
Copy-paste implementation of a tutorial model. No modifications made to layer depth or width.
Model is vastly over-parameterized (massive Transformer for tiny dataset) or under-parameterized for the task complexity.
Correct application of standard modern architectures (e.g., ResNet, LSTM, VAE). The model runs and converges.
Evidence of thoughtful configuration. Layers, stride, or kernel sizes are adjusted based on input resolution or sequence length.
Justifies choice of variants (e.g., using Bi-Directional LSTM vs standard, or ResNet50 vs ResNet18) based on resource/accuracy trade-offs.
Implements specialized architectural features (e.g., Attention mechanisms, Skip connections, or specific weight initializations) to solve specific problems.
Modifies the architecture for system constraints (e.g., Flash Attention, Quantization awareness, or pruning) or modifies the loss function.
Introduces a novel custom block or architectural modification. The design demonstrates a deep understanding of inductive bias.
No references provided.
Citations are from non-academic blogs or unrelated fields.
Relies entirely on foundational papers (e.g., 2012-2015) without acknowledging recent advancements.
Lists papers without connecting them. Fails to define the specific sub-niche (e.g., Object Detection) clearly.
Mentions architectures (e.g., "We used BERT") but does not cite the original paper or explain why it was a breakthrough.
Cites modern neural techniques relevant to the domain. Sets the context for the project.
Compares different families of models (e.g., "CNN vs Transformer for Vision") and explains the trade-offs found in literature.
Clearly identifies what existing models struggle with (e.g., "Transformers struggle with long-context without modification").
Follows conference paper format (Abstract, Intro, Methods). Review is critical, not just descriptive.
Synthesizes concepts from different domains (e.g., applying NLP attention mechanisms to Time Series).
A thorough review of the State of the Art (SOTA). The project is positioned as a logical next step in the research lineage.
Raw data used. Overfitting is rampant and ignored.
Data leakage present (e.g., test set used in training/validation loop).
Simple split (Train/Test) used. No validation set or cross-validation strategy.
Regularization (Dropout) added only after the model failed completely, without strategy.
Basic scaling and standard batch sizes used. No augmentation.
Effective use of regularization (Dropout, Early Stopping, Weight Decay) to handle noise. Standard Augmentation applied.
Handles class imbalance explicitly (Weighted Loss, Oversampling). Augmentation is visualized and verified.
Data pipeline is optimized (e.g., tf.data or Dataloaders with pre-fetching). Pre-processing aligns with pre-trained model requirements.
Advanced handling of representation (e.g., Embeddings, Tokenization strategy). Uses advanced augmentation (MixUp, CutMix).
Implements Curriculum Learning or sophisticated sampling strategies (Hard Negative Mining).
Handles heterogeneous data or creates a novel self-supervised learning task to boost data efficiency.
No metrics reported.
Reports training accuracy only. No test/validation metrics.
Uses wrong metric for task (e.g., Accuracy for highly imbalanced medical data instead of F1/AUC).
Reports a single final number. No learning curves (Loss/Epoch plots) shown.
Shows loss curves but does not interpret them (e.g., ignores obvious divergence or oscillation).
Uses appropriate metrics (Perplexity for NLP, Dice for Segmentation). Learning curves are interpreted correctly.
Compares results against a trivial baseline (e.g., "Always predict zero") to prove model value.
Uses Confusion Matrices or error analysis to identify specific classes where the model struggles.
Tests model robustness (e.g., adding noise to input, adversarial attacks, or out-of-distribution testing).
Performs Ablation Studies (removing parts of the model to see what contributes to performance).
Implements interpretability techniques (Grad-CAM, SHAP, Attention Maps) to validate the model is learning valid features.
No explanation of mechanics.
Fundamental misunderstanding of Backpropagation or Activation functions.
Uses terms like "Vanishing Gradient" without understanding how the architecture solves it.
Generic explanations of "Neural Networks" rather than the specific architecture used.
Explains the "What" (layers used) but not the "Why" (math behind the layers).
Clear explanation of model mechanics (e.g., how Convolutions work, or the Attention formula).
Discusses the optimizer choice (Adam vs SGD) and learning rate schedules (Warmup/Decay).
Explains specific mechanisms (e.g., "Why Residual connections aid gradient flow").
Deep technical mastery; explains mathematical nuances (e.g., distribution shifts in Batch Norm).
Can derive the loss function gradients or explain the information-theoretic basis of the model.
Connects the model design to first-principles math (Linear Algebra/Calculus). discusses the Loss Landscape geometry.
